name: Gesture Intent
summary: A standalone recognized-gesture vocabulary (placement ratified #1396) — the declarative layer between raw Pointer Events and the UX action a gesture drives (swipe to dismiss, long-press to act, pinch to zoom). Per-element and plural (mirrors focus-delegation / hover-intent), composing the Interaction Intent gated to pointer/touch modality. UX-only: the recognizer engine (native Pointer-Events math vs Hammer vs @use-gesture) is a DI/behavioral seam + a Technical Configurator card, NOT part of this intent. Carries one FIXED a11y invariant (WCAG 2.5.1): every gesture binding declares a single-pointer / keyboard equivalent — a gesture is never the only path.

vocabulary.description: The recognized gesture an element binds — the declarative "what" (this card is swipe-dismissable, this image is pinch-zoomable, this row is long-press-actionable). The listed values are the convergent core set across web (Hammer.js, @use-gesture, interact.js) and native (UIGestureRecognizer, SwiftUI Gesture, RN Gesture Handler, Compose), not a closed enum — this is an open-numbered axis (intents are open-design), so authors may mint additional recognizers per the variant contract. A binding is opt-in; no gesture is mandated and the a11y equivalent is always present (the restriction — "gesture-only" — is forbidden, never a value here).

direction.description: The constraint on a path-based gesture (swipe / pan / edge-swipe) — which axis or heading the recognizer accepts, mirroring the convergent Hammer DIRECTION_* / @use-gesture axis lock. Optional with a most-permissive default: absent → unconstrained (any direction). The listed values are the recommended core; open-numbered per the variant contract. Only meaningful for path gestures; ignored by discrete gestures (tap, long-press, pinch, rotate).

description:
What This Is

A standalone recognized-gesture vocabulary (placement ratified in #1396): the declarative layer between raw Pointer Events and the UX action a gesture drives. The platform ships the raw input (Pointer Events) and the opt-out (touch-action) but no native gesture recognizer and no gesture-event vocabulary — every incumbent ships a JS recognizer, the library tell. This intent owns the declaration (this element swipes / pinches / long-presses); the recognizer engine is a swappable impl.

Per-element & plural — why a standalone intent

A gesture binding is a per-element, plural declaration (this card swipes, that image pinches), whereas Interaction's modality is an ambient, document-level, singular value (what device the user is on). Folding plural per-element bindings into the ambient-singular modality intent conflates two scopes — so gesture is its own intent that composes interaction (gated to pointer/touch modality), exactly as Focus Delegation and Hover Intent carve interaction-adjacent concerns into their own intents.

Dimensions (UX-only)

  • vocabulary: the recognized gesture — tap · double-tap · long-press · pan · swipe · pinch · rotate · two-finger-pan · edge-swipe · pull-to-refresh, the convergent core, open-numbered (authors extend).
  • direction: for path gestures, the accepted axis/heading (horizontal · vertical · up · down · left · right); absent → unconstrained (the most-permissive default).

Fixed a11y invariant (not a dimension)

A gesture is never the only path. Every binding declares a single-pointer / keyboard equivalent — codified, not invented: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.5.1 Pointer Gestures (multipoint/path-based gestures need a single-pointer non-path equivalent), SC 2.5.2 Pointer Cancellation (fire on up, abortable), SC 2.5.4 Motion Actuation (a conventional-control equivalent). This rhymes with hover-intent's touch dimension, which exists precisely because hover has no touch equivalent — gestures carry the mirror obligation. Because it is a fixed invariant (a ratify), it is not exposed as a dimension.

UX-only → the engine is a Configurator card

The how — native Pointer-Events math vs Hammer.js vs @use-gesture, plus the touch-action / overscroll-behavior opt-outs — is a technical strategy, not part of this intent. The recognizer is behavioral (resolvable via the Ambient Intent / DI channel, native-first default) and authored as a Technical Configurator domain, mirroring hover-intent. A full Gesture Recognizer Protocol is a deferred, separately-prioritized candidate (no interop need today), not minted here.

The recognition ↔ effect seam

Gesture owns recognition; the effect a gesture drives lives elsewhere and composes: pinch → viewport transform (#1393), swipe → carousel paging, long-press → command / contextmenu, pan/drag → data-transfer / reorder. None is a conflict; all are compositions. Grounded in the placement sweep (pointer-gesture-vocabulary placement).
